unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: In this unit you meet the people who live and work in your neighbourhood, and you find out what their jobs are. You learn twenty-one words for helpers, for their special clothes and equipment, and for the actions they do, from the window cleaner and the bus driver to the firefighter, the nurse and the farmer. You learn to ask questions with What and Who, to count people and objects, and to say what somebody is doing right now with is or are and a verb ending in -ing. By the end you can describe a job, name the equipment that keeps a worker safe, and be a good neighbour like the children in this unit's story, The Helpers of Warta Street.
unit.learningPath:
Watch the teacher lecture, then learn a few vocabulary words each day and say each helper's job out loud.
Work through the six grammar practices: choosing and building What and Who questions, then is or are, the -ing spelling rules and full present continuous sentences.
Listen to the firefighter text, the poem My Neighbourhood, the interview with Leila and the story The Helpers of Warta Street, and answer the twenty-one comprehension questions.
Complete the six speaking, six writing and six activity practices.
Join three live teacher sessions in each of the two weeks.
Finish with the ten-question checkpoint and the self-assessment.
unit.origin: Rebuilt from the Year 2 Unit 2 source pack under docs/lesson-authoring-super-prompt.md
unit.reviewStatus: Pilot rebuild - pending curriculum reviewer sign-off

visual.alt: Cartoon street scene with a window cleaner on a long pole, a blue bus and its driver, two firefighters in helmets and boots, a police officer on a bicycle, and a child waving from a window.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who lives near you, on your street or in your building.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Theo is our neighbour, and he lives in the blue house.
practiceSentences: Every morning our neighbour Theo waves to us from his gate.
Who is the new neighbour in the house on the corner?
“Come and meet our neighbour,” said Mum, opening the front door.
My neighbour lends us her ladder when we clean the windows.
A good neighbour helps you carry your shopping up the stairs.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - e - i - g - h - b - o - u - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with your helper and tell the tutor the name of one neighbour and one kind thing that person does for your family.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person whose job is to clean the windows of houses and shops.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The window cleaner carries a bucket, a cloth and a very long pole.
practiceSentences: Our window cleaner comes on the first Monday of every month.
How does the window cleaner reach the windows on the top floor?
“Good morning!” called the window cleaner from the top of his ladder.
I like watching the window cleaner make the glass shine like water.
After the storm, the window cleaner washed mud off every shop window.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: w - i - n - d - o - w / c - l - e - a - n - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask the tutor how a window cleaner reaches the top windows of a tall shop.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who keeps people safe and helps everyone follow the rules.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The police officer stopped the traffic so the children could cross.
practiceSentences: Every school morning the police officer stands at the crossing in a bright jacket.
Where does the police officer keep her whistle and her badge?
“Please wait here until the road is clear,” said the police officer.
I asked the police officer the way to the post office.
When the market grew busy, the police officer helped an old man cross the road.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - o - l - i - c - e / o - f - f - i - c - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor, while your helper listens, to explain two ways a police officer keeps children safe near a busy road.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who finds out news and writes or tells stories about it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The reporter wrote about market day for the town newspaper.
practiceSentences: The reporter filled two whole pages of her notebook with names and numbers.
Does the reporter ask questions before she writes her story?
“May I take a photograph of your shop?” asked the reporter.
I want to be a reporter and tell people good news about our street.
On sports day a reporter came to watch our school running race.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - p - o - r - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper present, ask the tutor to interview you like a reporter about one thing that happened on your street.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person whose job is to drive a bus and carry passengers safely.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bus driver takes people from our town to the city every day.
practiceSentences: Our bus driver knows the name of every child on the school bus.
What time does the bus driver reach the stop by the market?
“Hold on tight, we are moving now,” said the bus driver.
I say good morning to the bus driver before I find my seat.
When the road was flooded, the bus driver found a safer way home.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - u - s / d - r - i - v - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask your adult helper to sit with you, then ask the tutor what a bus driver must check before the bus leaves the stop.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who puts out fires and rescues people from danger.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The firefighter climbed the ladder and carried the frightened cat down.
practiceSentences: Two firefighters ran towards the smoke while everybody else moved back.
How long does a firefighter train before the first day at work?
“Stand well back, please,” the firefighter said to the crowd.
My cousin Noah wants to be a firefighter when he grows up.
A firefighter visited our class and showed us the fire engine hose.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - i - r - e - f - i - g - h - t - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask the tutor to name three things a firefighter does on a busy day.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A hard hat that protects your head from knocks and falling things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The firefighter wears a helmet to keep her head safe.
practiceSentences: The yellow helmet has a small light on the front for dark rooms.
Where did you leave your bicycle helmet, Sami?
“Put your helmet on before you ride,” said Dad.
My helmet feels heavy at first, but I soon forget it is there.
Builders wear a helmet on the building site all day long.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - l - m - e - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Show your helper where a helmet sits, then ask the tutor which other workers wear one and why.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Strong shoes that cover your feet and part of your legs.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Firefighters wear heavy boots so that hot ground cannot hurt their feet.
practiceSentences: The firefighter pulled on her boots in less than ten seconds.
Are your boots still wet from the rain, Nora?
“Leave your muddy boots by the door,” said Grandma Hana.
I wear my old boots when I help Mum in the garden.
The farmer's boots were covered in soft brown mud from the field.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - o - o - t - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper present, ask the tutor why firefighters need heavy boots and not soft shoes.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Coverings for your hands, with a separate part for each finger.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She wears thick gloves so that the heat cannot burn her hands.
practiceSentences: The firefighter's gloves are thick enough to hold a hot metal handle.
Why does the nurse put on clean gloves before she helps someone?
“These gloves are far too big for me!” laughed Mina.
I keep my warm gloves in my coat pocket all winter.
Gardeners wear gloves to protect their hands from sharp thorns.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - l - o - v - e - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit beside your adult helper and ask the tutor to name two jobs where gloves keep hands safe.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A covering that goes over your nose and mouth to protect them.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The firefighter wears a mask so that she can breathe fresh air in smoke.
practiceSentences: The mask is joined to an air tank that the firefighter carries on her back.
Why does the doctor wear a mask when she treats a patient?
“I cannot hear you through your mask,” said Leo, leaning closer.
I made a paper mask of a lion for our class play.
A painter wears a mask so that the dust stays out of her nose.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: m - a - s - k
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult next to you, ask the tutor how a mask helps a firefighter breathe inside thick smoke.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The special set of clothes that people wear for their job or school.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The bus driver wears a blue uniform with silver buttons.
practiceSentences: Every nurse at the clinic wears the same green uniform.
Is your school uniform grey, or is it dark blue?
“Your uniform looks very smart today,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I hang my school uniform on the chair before I go to bed.
You can find a helper quickly because a uniform is easy to see.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: u - n - i - f - o - r - m
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Describe your school uniform to the tutor while your helper listens, then ask how it is different from a nurse's uniform.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who helps people who are ill and makes them better.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Doctor Sarah listened to my chest and said I would soon be well.
practiceSentences: Doctor Sarah works at the clinic beside the post office.
When did the doctor last check your ears, Maya?
“Open wide and say ah,” said the doctor with a smile.
My little brother Idris was not afraid of the doctor at all.
The doctor wrote a note so that I could rest at home for two days.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - o - c - t - o - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper beside you, ask the tutor what questions a doctor asks a patient who feels unwell.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who cares for people who are ill or hurt.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The nurse cleaned my knee and put a soft bandage on it.
practiceSentences: The nurse weighed the baby and wrote the number in a small blue book.
How many hours does a nurse work in one long night?
“You were very brave today,” the nurse told Mina.
I want to thank the nurse who looked after my grandmother.
At the school clinic a nurse checks our eyes every year.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: n - u - r - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor, with an adult present, to explain one thing a nurse does that a doctor does not do.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person whose job is to help other people learn new things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Teacher Yasmin helps us read new English words every morning.
practiceSentences: Our teacher writes the new words on the board in big round letters.
Which teacher looks after the school library on Fridays?
“Take out your reading books, please,” said the teacher.
I showed my teacher the story I wrote about our street.
A teacher plans the lessons long before the children arrive.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - a - c - h - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with your helper and tell the tutor one new thing your teacher taught you this week.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who grows food and looks after farm animals.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The farmer grows maize and keeps six brown goats.
practiceSentences: The farmer fills the water trough before the sun gets hot.
How early does the farmer wake up to milk the cows?
“Mind the wet path,” the farmer said as we walked into the field.
I helped a farmer pick beans at my uncle's farm last year.
Every market day the farmer brings fresh vegetables into town.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: f - a - r - m - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult beside you, ask the tutor which foods a farmer near your town might grow.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A person who owns or looks after a shop and sells things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Omar the shopkeeper sold us warm bread and sweet oranges.
practiceSentences: The shopkeeper stacks the tins neatly on the shelf each evening.
Does the shopkeeper open the door before eight o'clock?
“That will be five coins, please,” said the shopkeeper.
I counted my money carefully and gave it to the shopkeeper.
When the rain started, the shopkeeper pulled a cover over the fruit.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - h - o - p - k - e - e - p - e - r
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper present, practise buying bread from the tutor as if it were the shopkeeper, then ask for feedback.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Doing something that makes a job easier or better for someone.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Adam is helping our neighbour carry a heavy box of books.
practiceSentences: Nora is helping her little sister find both of her shoes.
Who is helping the shopkeeper sweep the smoke out of the shop?
“Thank you for helping me,” said Omar warmly.
I am helping Mum wash the plates after our meal.
The whole street is helping, so the work is finished quickly.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - e - l - p - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Sit beside your adult helper and say one sentence that begins with I am helping, then ask the tutor to check it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Showing someone how to do something so that they can learn it.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Teacher Yasmin is teaching us five new words about jobs.
practiceSentences: Karim is teaching his son how to hold the long pole safely.
What is the firefighter teaching the class about fire safety?
“I am teaching you a song about helpers,” said Teacher Yasmin.
I am teaching my brother Idris the names of the colours.
Omar is teaching Amal how to count the change in the shop.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - e - a - c - h - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, tell the tutor about something you are teaching a younger child, and ask for one new idea.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Saving a person or an animal from danger and bringing them somewhere safe.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The firefighters are rescuing the baby goats behind the market.
practiceSentences: Two firefighters are rescuing a family from the smoky building.
Who is rescuing the kitten that is stuck in the tall tree?
“They are rescuing everybody now,” said Rami calmly.
I am rescuing my paper boat before the water carries it away.
Rescuing people is one of the bravest parts of the job.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - s - c - u - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor, while your helper listens, to describe a rescue at the market and to use the word rescuing twice.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Making plants get bigger by giving them water, sunlight and care.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The farmer is growing beans in the field beside the road.
practiceSentences: Our class is growing tomatoes in three pots by the window.
What is the farmer growing in the big field this year?
“The maize is growing fast after the rain,” said the farmer.
I am growing a small plant from one orange seed.
Growing food takes patience, water and plenty of hard work.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: g - r - o - w - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With your helper beside you, tell the tutor what you are growing at home or at school, and ask one question about it.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Making a car, bus or lorry move and steering it along the road.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Nadia is driving the blue bus to the city this morning.
practiceSentences: The bus driver is driving slowly because the market road is busy.
Where is Nadia driving the bus after she leaves our stop?
“I am driving to the city,” said Nadia through her open window.
I am driving my toy tractor across the kitchen floor.
Driving a bus needs careful eyes and two steady hands.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: d - r - i - v - i - n - g
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: With an adult present, ask the tutor to describe where Nadia is driving her bus, then say the sentence back.

rubrics.level1: Hard to hear or to follow
rubrics.level3: Mostly clear and easy to follow

rubrics.level1: Few job or equipment words used
rubrics.level2: One or two job words, usually prompted
rubrics.level3: Most job and equipment words used correctly
rubrics.level4: Wide range of job and equipment words used naturally

rubrics.level1: Cannot yet form a What or Who question
rubrics.level2: Forms a question with full support
rubrics.level3: Forms What and Who questions with few errors
rubrics.level4: Asks and answers What and Who questions accurately

rubrics.level1: Leaves out is or are and the -ing ending
rubrics.level2: Uses one correct is or are sentence
rubrics.level3: Uses several correct is and are sentences
rubrics.level4: Chooses is and are accurately every time, with correct -ing forms

rubrics.level1: The job is not yet clear
rubrics.level2: Names the job with one detail
rubrics.level3: Names the job with two or three real details
rubrics.level4: Names the job with rich, accurate detail about duties and equipment

rubrics.level1: Needs support to order the sentences
rubrics.level4: Sentences follow a clear order and link smoothly

rubrics.level1: Very few unit words or patterns
rubrics.level2: Some unit words, patterns still forming
rubrics.level3: Mostly accurate unit words and present continuous
rubrics.level4: Accurate and varied unit words, questions and present continuous

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and full stops need support
rubrics.level4: Every sentence is punctuated correctly, including question marks

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name the different people who live and work in your neighbourhood.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five neighbourhood helpers named aloud in Speaking 6, and at least four real helpers named in the Writing 2 poem.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Describe a job and explain what the person does in it.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Three accurate job sentences in Writing 1, the five matched pairs in Activity 1, and a recorded job description in Speaking 1.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Ask and answer questions using What and Who.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct choices in Grammar 1, six self-made questions in Grammar 3, and six recorded questions in Speaking 4.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Count people and objects, and report the number in a sentence.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: The ten passengers counted correctly in comprehension question 15, and the numbers recorded in the firefighter listening report.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Say and write what someone is doing right now, using is or are with verb+ing.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Five correct is or are choices in Grammar 2, six spoken action sentences in Speaking 5, and six written pairs in Activity 6.

outcomes.learningOutcome: Name the special clothes and equipment used in different jobs, and say what each one protects.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Four correct equipment words in Activity 4, the ten sorted items in Activity 5, and a labelled fact card in Writing 4.

readings.title: Firefighters and Their Equipment
readings.passageScript: Firefighters have important jobs. They put out fires. They rescue people who are trapped inside burning buildings. Firefighters wear special clothes that keep them safe. They wear heavy boots on their feet. They wear helmets to keep their heads safe. A firefighter wears a mask on the face to breathe fresh air, and thick gloves to hold hot things.

readings.title: A Poem: “My Neighbourhood”
readings.passageScript:
Come and meet the people in my neighbourhood.
There are neighbours helping neighbours in my neighbourhood.
There are grandmas and grandpas,
Mums and dads, children too,
In my neighbourhood, the streets around my home.

readings.title: An Interview with a Firefighter
readings.passageScript:
Listen to Leila, a firefighter. Ask an adult to read her words aloud, and then read them again by yourself.
“I am a firefighter. I work at the fire station. When there is a fire, I ride on the fire engine. I use water to fight the fire. I also visit schools and talk to children about fire safety.”

readings.title: The Helpers of Warta Street
readings.genre: A gentle story about neighbours, with plenty of talking.
readings.theme: Good neighbours, community helpers, and everyday kindness.
readings.setting: A busy little town, on the street beside the market.
readings.passageScript:
Amal lived on Warta Street, near the market. She loved her street because everyone helped each other. The people who lived near her were her neighbours, and they were kind.
One hot morning, Amal sat by her window. She watched the busy street.
“Look, Mum,” she said. “The window cleaner is here!”
A tall man named Karim was cleaning the windows of the shop next door. He used a long pole to reach the high glass.
“Good morning, Amal!” Karim called. “What are you doing today?”
“I am watching all the helpers,” said Amal. “Who else works on our street?”
Karim smiled. “Many good people work here,” he said. “Look, here comes the bus driver.”
A big blue bus stopped at the corner. The bus driver, Nadia, waved. She wore a neat blue uniform.
“Hello, Amal!” said Nadia. “I am driving to the city. Do you want to count my passengers?”
Amal laughed and counted. “One, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, nine, ten! There are ten people on your bus!”
Just then, a loud bell rang. Amal saw smoke behind the market.
“A fire!” shouted Karim. “Everyone, stay back!”
A red fire engine came fast down the street. Two firefighters jumped out. They wore helmets on their heads, heavy boots on their feet, and thick gloves on their hands. One firefighter, Leila, wore a mask on her face so that she could breathe.
“What are they doing?” asked Amal.
“They are putting out the fire,” said her mother. “And look, they are rescuing the baby goats!”
The firefighters used water to fight the fire. Soon the smoke was gone. Everyone on Warta Street cheered.
A police officer named Rami arrived on his bicycle. He kept the people safe.
“Please stand back, my friends,” said Rami kindly. “The market is safe now. Thank you for helping each other.”
That is when Amal saw old Omar. Omar sold bread and fruit in the market. His small shop was full of smoke, and he looked sad.
“Mum, we must help him,” said Amal.
So Amal and her mother ran to help. Karim brought water. Nadia swept the floor. Rami carried the heavy boxes. Even the two firefighters helped clean the shop.
“Thank you, my good neighbours,” said Omar, wiping his eyes. “You all have important jobs, but today you helped an old shopkeeper. That is the kindest job of all.”
Omar gave everyone fresh bread. It was warm and soft.
That night, Amal told her father about the day. “A window cleaner, a bus driver, two firefighters, a police officer,” she said. “Everyone on Warta Street helps.”
“Yes,” said her father. “And so do you. A good neighbour is the best helper of all.”
Amal smiled and closed her eyes. Tomorrow she would sit by her window again, and maybe help a neighbour too.

comprehension.question: What two jobs does the text say firefighters do?
comprehension.correctAnswer: They put out fires, and they rescue people who are trapped inside burning buildings.
comprehension.explanation: Both jobs are in the second and third sentences of the text, one job in each sentence.

comprehension.question: Which four pieces of special clothing does the text say firefighters wear?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Heavy boots, helmets, a mask and thick gloves.
comprehension.explanation: The text lists the equipment in order from the feet upwards, and finishes with the gloves.

comprehension.question: Why does a firefighter need a mask, and why does she need gloves?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The mask lets her breathe fresh air inside the smoke, and the gloves let her hold hot things without burning her hands.
comprehension.explanation: The last sentence gives both reasons: fresh air for the mask, and hot things for the gloves.

comprehension.question: Draw and label a firefighter. Beside each label, write the part of the body that the equipment keeps safe.
comprehension.correctAnswer: Helmet keeps the head safe, mask covers the face for breathing, gloves protect the hands, and boots protect the feet.
comprehension.explanation: Every label must come from the text, and each one must be joined to the right part of the body.

comprehension.question: Which five kinds of people does the poem say live in the neighbourhood?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Grandmas, grandpas, mums, dads and children.
comprehension.explanation: Lines three and four of the poem list all five together.

comprehension.question: What does the poem say the neighbours are doing?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The neighbours are helping other neighbours.
comprehension.explanation: Line two of the poem says that neighbours are helping neighbours.

comprehension.question: Which four words are repeated in almost every line, and what new line could you add about your own street?
comprehension.correctAnswer: The words in my neighbourhood are repeated. A new line could be: There are helpers waving in my neighbourhood.
comprehension.explanation: Spotting the repeated phrase shows how the poem is built, and the new line must keep the same pattern.

comprehension.question: Leila is a ______.
comprehension.correctAnswer: firefighter
comprehension.explanation: Leila says this in her very first sentence.

comprehension.question: She works at the ______.
comprehension.correctAnswer: fire station
comprehension.explanation: Her second sentence names her workplace.

comprehension.question: When there is a fire, she ______ on the fire engine.
comprehension.correctAnswer: rides
comprehension.explanation: Listen for the verb after the words when there is a fire.

comprehension.question: She uses ______ to fight the fire.
comprehension.correctAnswer: water
comprehension.explanation: Leila names only one thing that she uses to fight the fire.

comprehension.question: She also visits ______ and talks to children about ______.
comprehension.correctAnswer: schools; fire safety
comprehension.explanation: The last sentence holds both answers, one before and one after the word about.

comprehension.question: On which street does Amal live, and what stands close to her home?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Amal lives on Warta Street, and the market stands close to her home.
comprehension.explanation: The very first sentence of the story gives the street and the market.

comprehension.question: What is Karim's job, and what does he use to reach the high glass?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Karim is a window cleaner, and he uses a long pole to reach the high glass.
comprehension.explanation: Amal names his job from her window, and the next paragraph names his long pole.

comprehension.question: How many passengers does Amal count on Nadia's bus?
comprehension.correctAnswer: She counts ten passengers.
comprehension.explanation: Amal counts out loud from one to ten and then says the total.

comprehension.question: Which four pieces of equipment do the two firefighters wear in the story?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Helmets on their heads, heavy boots on their feet, thick gloves on their hands, and a mask on Leila's face.
comprehension.explanation: One long sentence lists three items, and the sentence after it adds Leila's mask.

comprehension.question: How did the neighbours help Omar after the fire?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Karim brought water, Nadia swept the floor, Rami carried the heavy boxes, and the two firefighters helped clean the shop.
comprehension.explanation: Four helpers are named in one short paragraph, each with a different action.

comprehension.question: Why did Omar call helping him the kindest job of all?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Because the helpers had already done their own paid jobs that day, and they chose to help him only out of kindness.
comprehension.explanation: Omar contrasts their important jobs with the free choice they made to help one old shopkeeper.

comprehension.question: Which community helpers appear in this story, and which one would you like to be? Give one reason.
comprehension.correctAnswer: The story shows a window cleaner, a bus driver, two firefighters, a police officer and a shopkeeper. Then any one of them, with a reason such as: I would like to be a firefighter because I want to rescue people.
comprehension.explanation: The list must come from the story, and the choice must be followed by a reason using because.

comprehension.question: Amal's father said that a good neighbour is the best helper of all. What does he mean?
comprehension.correctAnswer: He means that anyone can notice a need and help kindly, and that helping does not have to be part of a paid job.
comprehension.explanation: Link his words back to the moment when the whole street cleaned Omar's shop for nothing.

comprehension.question: How do you help your neighbours, and how do they help your family?
comprehension.correctAnswer: Two honest examples, one in each direction, such as: I carry the shopping for the family next door, and they water our plants when we are away.
comprehension.explanation: Both halves must be answered, and each example should be a real one from the child's own street.

grammar.title: What and Who: Choosing the Question Word
grammar.explanation: We use question words to ask for information. What asks about a thing, an action or a job. Who asks about a person. These two little words help you find out about the helpers in your neighbourhood.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use What for a thing, an action or a job.
What is this?
What is the police officer doing?
What is your job?
Use Who for a person.
Who is your neighbour?
Who drives the bus?
grammar.commonMistake: Some children write, Who is the firefighter doing? An action is a thing and not a person, so the question should be, What is the firefighter doing?
grammar.memoryTip: Who has the letter o in it, just like the o in pers-o-n. If you are asking about a person, choose who.
grammar.practice:
Write What or Who to start each question.
1. ______ is the bus driver? (You want to know the person.)
2. ______ is the doctor doing?
3. ______ is helping the old man?
4. ______ does a firefighter wear?
5. ______ is your teacher?

grammar.title: What and Who: Building Your Own Questions
grammar.explanation: Once you can choose the question word, you can build the whole question. Put the question word first, keep the other words in question order, and always finish with a question mark.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Question word, then the helping word or the verb, then the rest.
Who helps people who are ill? A doctor helps people who are ill.
What is the farmer growing? The farmer is growing maize.
Who drives the school bus? Nadia drives the school bus.
grammar.commonMistake: It is easy to write, Who the bus drives? The words have slipped out of order, so the question should be, Who drives the bus?
grammar.memoryTip: Question word first, question mark last. Say the whole question out loud before you write it down.
grammar.practice:
Write one What question and one Who question about each picture.
1. A firefighter holding a hose.
2. A nurse helping a child.
3. A farmer working in a field.

grammar.title: Actions Happening Now: Is or Are
grammar.explanation: The present continuous tells us about an action that is happening right now. It helps you describe what the people in your neighbourhood are doing at this very moment.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
The pattern is subject, then is or are, then the verb with -ing.
Use is with he, she and it.
He is driving the bus.
She is helping the patient.
Use are with you, we and they.
You are learning English.
We are helping our neighbours.
They are putting out the fire.
grammar.commonMistake: Children often write, The firefighters is climbing the ladder. Firefighters means more than one person, so the sentence should be, The firefighters are climbing the ladder.
grammar.memoryTip: One person or thing takes is. More than one takes are. And never forget the -ing on the action word.
grammar.practice:
Write is or are, then add the action word in the present continuous.
1. The police officer ______ (help) the children.
2. The firefighters ______ (climb) the ladder.
3. My neighbour ______ (clean) the windows.
4. We ______ (learn) about jobs.
5. The farmer ______ (grow) maize.

grammar.title: Building the -ing Form
grammar.explanation: Before you can use the present continuous, you need the -ing form of the action word. Most verbs simply add -ing, but a few change their spelling first.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Most verbs just add -ing, so help becomes helping and teach becomes teaching.
Verbs that end in a silent e drop the e first, so drive becomes driving and rescue becomes rescuing.
Short verbs with one vowel and one final consonant double the last letter, so run becomes running.
grammar.commonMistake: Writing driveing keeps the silent e. Drop the e before you add -ing, and the word becomes driving.
grammar.memoryTip: Look at the last letter first. Silent e says goodbye, and a short verb doubles its final letter for company.
grammar.practice:
Write the -ing form of each action word.
1. help
2. teach
3. rescue
4. drive
5. clean
6. run

grammar.title: Actions Happening Now: Putting the Sentence Together
grammar.explanation: A complete present continuous sentence needs three parts: the person or thing, the helping word is or are, and the action word ending in -ing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Person or thing, then is or are, then the verb with -ing.
The window cleaner is washing the high glass.
The neighbours are carrying the heavy boxes.
Nadia is driving the blue bus.
grammar.commonMistake: Writing, The nurse helping the child, leaves out the helping word. Put is in front of the -ing word and write, The nurse is helping the child.
grammar.memoryTip: Ask yourself three quick questions: who, is or are, and doing what?
grammar.practice:
Put the words in the right order and write each sentence.
1. is / Karim / cleaning / the window
2. are / the firefighters / using / water
3. is / Amal / counting / the passengers

grammar.title: Neighbourhood Reporter
grammar.explanation: A reporter tells people what is happening while it happens. In this task you report a busy neighbourhood scene, using is and are with action words that end in -ing.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Name the person, choose is or are, then say the action.
Nadia is driving the bus.
Two firefighters are putting out the fire.
Rami is keeping the people safe.
Omar and Amal are sweeping the shop.
grammar.commonMistake: Check the helping word against the number of people. Omar and Amal is sweeping names two people, so it becomes, Omar and Amal are sweeping.
grammar.memoryTip: Count the people before you choose. One person takes is, and two or more people take are.
grammar.practice: Write six sentences about a busy neighbourhood picture. Use is in three sentences and are in three sentences, and use six different action words.

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Describe a Job
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Choose one job from your word list and get ready to talk about it.
Say: A ______ helps people by ______.
Say: A ______ wears ______.
Say: A ______ uses ______.
Record yourself, then listen back. Did you name the job, say what the person does, and speak clearly?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Sit with your helper, describe your chosen job in three sentences, and ask the tutor whether you named both the clothes and the tools.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Ask and Answer About a Picture
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Find a picture of people at work, or use the neighbourhood picture in this unit.
For each person ask: What is the ______ doing? Then answer: The ______ is ______.
Next ask: Who is helping the ______? Then answer: ______ is helping.
Ask about at least four different people.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Describe one worker in your picture to the tutor, with an adult beside you, and ask it to reply with one What question and one Who question.

speaking.title: Speaking Game — Who Am I?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play this guessing game with an adult or a friend.
Think of a job and keep it secret. Act it out, such as putting out a fire, driving a bus or cleaning a window.
The other player asks: Are you a ______?
Answer: Yes, I am! or No, I am not. Swap turns after three guesses.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to think of a job while your helper watches, guess it in five questions, and then swap roles.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Who and What Detective
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Look at the busy Warta Street picture with a partner.
Ask three Who questions and three What questions about the people you can see.
Model: Who is driving the bus? Nadia is driving. What is Karim doing? He is cleaning a window.
Record all six questions together with their answers.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Read your six questions to the tutor while your helper listens, and ask which of them needed Who and which needed What.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — What Is Happening Now?
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Describe six actions in the neighbourhood picture, one after the other.
Use is or are with an action word ending in -ing.
Model: The firefighter is carrying a hose. The neighbours are helping Omar.
Record your six sentences and check that every one has a helping word.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Say your six action sentences to the tutor with an adult present, and ask it to name any sentence that is missing is or are.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — Be a Good Neighbour
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Role-play meeting a neighbour who needs help.
One of you carries something heavy. The other asks what happened and offers help.
Use these lines: Your box looks heavy. May I help you carry it? Thank you, that is very kind.
Swap roles, then record your best version.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Ask the tutor to play a neighbour with a heavy bag while your helper sits with you, then ask for feedback on how politely you offered help.

writing.title: Writing 1 — Three Sentences About a Job
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose one job. Write three sentences about it: what the person does, what the person wears, and how the person helps your neighbourhood.
writing.modelText: A firefighter puts out fires and rescues people. A firefighter wears a helmet, heavy boots and thick gloves. A firefighter keeps our whole street safe.
writing.successCriteria: My three sentences name the job, the clothes and the help. Every sentence begins with a capital letter and ends with a full stop.
writing.support: Use the Jobs and Equipment word list, and say each sentence aloud before you write it.
writing.extension: Add one more sentence beginning with the word Because, to explain why the job matters to your street.

writing.title: Writing 2 — Your Own Neighbourhood Poem
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write your own version of the poem My Neighbourhood. Keep the first line and the last line, and change the middle lines so that they fit your own street. Then draw a picture to go with your poem.
writing.modelText:
Come and meet the people in my neighbourhood.
There are neighbours helping neighbours in my neighbourhood.
There are drivers and cleaners,
Nurses and teachers, children too,
In my neighbourhood, the streets around my home.
writing.sentenceStarter: Come and meet the people in my neighbourhood.
writing.successCriteria: My poem has five lines, keeps the first and the last line of the original, and names at least four real helpers from my street.
writing.support: List four helpers you really know before you start, then fit them into the middle lines.
writing.extension: Read your poem aloud to your family, then add one line about how the helpers make you feel.

writing.title: Writing 3 — Story Response: The Helpers of Warta Street
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write three sentences about helpers on your own street, beginning each one with: On my street, the ______ helps by ______. Then draw the helpers of Warta Street and write one present continuous sentence under each person.
writing.modelText:
On my street, the shopkeeper helps by keeping the shop open late. On my street, the bus driver helps by waiting for the slow walkers. On my street, the window cleaner helps by washing the school windows.
Karim is cleaning the high glass. Nadia is driving the blue bus. Rami is keeping everybody safe.
writing.successCriteria: My three street sentences use the given opening, and every drawing label uses is with an action word ending in -ing.
writing.support: Reread the paragraph where the neighbours clean Omar's shop, and notice what each helper did.
writing.extension: Write one sentence about a helper you would like to thank, and say why you would thank that person.

writing.title: Writing 4 — A Community Helper Fact Card
writing.promptAndInstructions: Make a fact card about one community helper. Give the card a heading with the job name, write two duties, and name two pieces of clothing or equipment. Add a small labelled drawing.
writing.modelText:
Nurse
A nurse cares for people who are ill or hurt. A nurse cleans cuts and puts on clean bandages. A nurse wears a green uniform and clean gloves.
writing.successCriteria: My card has a heading, two duties, two named pieces of clothing or equipment, and a labelled drawing.
writing.support: Use the More Jobs in the Community list and the model card as your plan.
writing.extension: Make a second card for a job that nobody else in your class has chosen.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Warta Street News Report
writing.promptAndInstructions: You are the reporter for the town newspaper. Write a short report about the fire on Warta Street. Say where it happened, who came to help, what the helpers did, and how the day ended.
writing.modelText: A fire started behind the market on Warta Street. A red fire engine arrived, and two firefighters jumped out. They put out the fire with water and rescued the baby goats. Then the whole street helped Omar clean his smoky shop. By evening, Warta Street was safe and calm again.
writing.successCriteria: My report answers where, who, what and how it ended, keeps the events in order, and uses only facts from the story.
writing.support: Use the order words first, next and finally to keep your events in the right order.
writing.extension: Add one sentence in which a helper speaks, and put speech marks around the words.

writing.title: Writing 6 — My Good-Neighbour Promise
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write six promises about how you can be a good neighbour at home, at school or on your street. Begin five promises with I can, and begin the last one with This week I will.
writing.modelText: I can greet my neighbours every morning. I can carry a bag for an older person. I can keep our shared path clean. I can listen when somebody needs help. I can share my books with the child next door. This week I will help sweep in front of our door.
writing.sentenceStarter: I can be a good neighbour by
writing.successCriteria: I wrote six different promises, five beginning with I can and one beginning with This week I will, and every promise ends with a full stop.
writing.support: Think about the morning, school time and the evening to find six different promises.
writing.extension: Explain in one extra sentence how one of your promises will help another person.

activities.title: Activity 1 — Match Jobs and Tools
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Match each job to the tools it uses. Write the pair, or draw a line between them.
Firefighter — ______
Police officer — ______
Doctor — ______
Teacher — ______
Farmer — ______
Choose from: hose and ladder, badge and whistle, stethoscope and medicine, books and board, tractor and seeds.
activities.answerSummary: Firefighter — hose and ladder. Police officer — badge and whistle. Doctor — stethoscope and medicine. Teacher — books and board. Farmer — tractor and seeds.

activities.title: Activity 2 — Fill in the Blanks (Job Descriptions)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Copy each sentence and fill both gaps.
1. A firefighter puts out ______ and rescues ______.
2. A police officer keeps people ______ and directs ______.
3. A doctor helps people who are ______ and gives them ______.
4. A teacher ______ students and helps them ______.
5. A farmer grows ______ and raises ______.
activities.answerSummary: 1. fires, people. 2. safe, traffic. 3. ill, medicine. 4. teaches, learn. 5. crops, animals.

activities.title: Activity 3 — True or False (Jobs and Actions)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False for each sentence. If it is false, write the correct sentence underneath.
1. Police officers help keep people safe.
2. Doctors grow crops and vegetables.
3. Bus drivers take people from place to place.
4. Teachers put out fires in buildings.
activities.answerSummary: 1. True. 2. False, because doctors help people who are ill, and farmers grow crops. 3. True. 4. False, because teachers teach students, and firefighters put out fires.

activities.title: Activity 4 — Fill in the Blanks (Firefighter Equipment)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Copy each sentence and fill the gap with the right piece of equipment.
1. Firefighters wear ______ on their heads to protect them.
2. They wear heavy ______ on their feet.
3. They wear ______ on their hands to protect them from heat.
4. A firefighter wears a ______ on the face to help her breathe.

activities.title: Activity 5 — Who Uses It?
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort these ten items under the correct helper: hose, helmet, stethoscope, medicine, books, board, badge, whistle, seeds, tractor.
activities.answerSummary: Firefighter: hose, helmet. Doctor: stethoscope, medicine. Teacher: books, board. Police officer: badge, whistle. Farmer: seeds, tractor.

activities.title: Activity 6 — Neighbourhood Action Hunt
activities.instructionsAndItems: Watch your own street, or look at the unit picture, and find six people who are helping. For each one, write the job and one sentence saying what the person is doing right now, using is or are with an action word ending in -ing.
activities.answerSummary: Six job-and-action pairs. Each sentence must name the helper, use is for one person and are for two or more, and end the action word with -ing, for example: The shopkeeper is stacking the shelves.

assignments.title: Good Neighbours and Jobs learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Put together three pieces of work about the helper you know best. First complete Writing 4 — A Community Helper Fact Card. Next record Speaking 1 — Describe a Job about the same helper. Finally complete Writing 6 — My Good-Neighbour Promise. Check all three against the writing and speaking rubrics before you submit them together.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does the word neighbour mean?
quizzes.options: A person who puts out fires and rescues people. | A person who owns a shop and sells things. | A person who lives near you. | A hard hat that protects the head.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A person who lives near you.
quizzes.explanation: A neighbour lives close to your home. The other options describe a firefighter, a shopkeeper and a helmet.

quizzes.question: The ______ uses a long pole to reach the high windows.
quizzes.options: window cleaner | helmet | uniform | busy market square
quizzes.explanation: Only a person can use a pole. A helmet and a uniform are clothing, and a market square is a place.

quizzes.question: Which piece of equipment helps a firefighter breathe fresh air inside smoke?
quizzes.options: A badge | A tractor | A whistle | A mask
quizzes.explanation: The mask covers the nose and mouth. A badge and a whistle belong to a police officer, and a tractor belongs to a farmer.

quizzes.question: Which of these questions asks about an action?
quizzes.options: Who is the bus driver? | What is the police officer doing? | Who is the firefighter helping today? | Who is your teacher?
quizzes.correctAnswer: What is the police officer doing?
quizzes.explanation: An action is a thing and not a person, so it takes What. The other three questions all ask about a person.

quizzes.question: The firefighters ______ climbing the ladder.
quizzes.explanation: Firefighters means more than one person, so the helping word is are. Am belongs with I, and is belongs with one person.

quizzes.question: Which is the correct -ing form of the verb drive?
quizzes.explanation: Drive ends in a silent e, so the e is dropped before -ing. Drived is not a word, and the v is never doubled.

quizzes.question: In the story, how many passengers does Amal count on Nadia's bus?
quizzes.explanation: Amal counts all the way from one to ten, then says that there are ten people on the bus.

quizzes.question: In the story, what does Karim use to reach the high glass?
quizzes.options: A ladder and a rope | A long pole | A hose and a bucket of water | A tractor and some seeds
quizzes.explanation: The story says Karim used a long pole. A hose belongs to the firefighters, and a tractor belongs to a farmer.

quizzes.question: The bus driver wears a blue ______ to work every day.
quizzes.explanation: A uniform is the set of clothes worn for a job. A helmet is worn by firefighters, and a tractor and a stethoscope are not clothes.

quizzes.question: What does the word rescuing mean?
quizzes.options: Selling bread and fruit in a small shop | Making plants get bigger with water and sun | Cleaning the high windows with a long pole | Saving someone from danger
quizzes.explanation: Rescuing means bringing someone out of danger. The other options describe a shopkeeper, a farmer and a window cleaner.

liveSessions.title: Meet the neighbourhood helpers
liveSessions.beforeSession: Watch the teacher lecture and learn Group 1 of the word list: neighbour, window cleaner, police officer, reporter and bus driver.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min welcome; 10 min picture talk about a busy street; 10 min vocabulary retrieval with job cards; 15 min paired job descriptions; 5 min reflection
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 1 — Describe a Job and add the five helper words to your word wall.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

liveSessions.title: Firefighters, equipment and safety
liveSessions.beforeSession: Listen to Firefighters and Their Equipment, and bring one question about fire safety.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min retrieval; 10 min shared reading of the firefighter text; 10 min equipment sorting onto a body outline; 15 min comprehension questions 1 to 4; 5 min safety exit ticket
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Activity 4 and improve two of your reading answers.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

liveSessions.title: Asking with What and Who
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete Grammar 1 and Grammar 3, and bring your six new questions.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min warm-up; 10 min sorting questions into What and Who; 10 min teacher modelling of question order; 15 min Who and What Detective in pairs; 5 min feedback
liveSessions.afterSession: Record Speaking 4 and correct any question that had the words out of order.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

liveSessions.title: Actions happening now
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete Grammar 2, 4, 5 and 6, and practise the five action words from Group 4.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min action mime; 10 min choosing is or are; 10 min building -ing forms; 15 min reporting a neighbourhood picture; 5 min review
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

liveSessions.title: The Helpers of Warta Street
liveSessions.beforeSession: Listen to the story once, and choose the helper you admire most.
liveSessions.agenda: 5 min prediction from the title; 10 min echo reading with attention to speech marks; 10 min sequencing the events of the fire; 15 min drafting the Warta Street news report; 5 min peer feedback
liveSessions.afterSession: Finish Writing 5 and check every fact against the story.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

liveSessions.title: Good-neighbour showcase and review
liveSessions.beforeSession: Complete the ten-question checkpoint and bring one portfolio piece.
liveSessions.agenda: 10 min retrieval game with job cards; 10 min checkpoint corrections; 10 min good-neighbour role-plays; 10 min portfolio sharing; 5 min self-assessment
liveSessions.afterSession: Complete Writing 6 and save your final self-assessment.
liveSessions.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

teacherNotes.noteType: AI tutor use and device safety
teacherNotes.note: Every AI tutor task in this unit assumes that an adult is present. Never leave a child alone with the device: sit beside them, keep the screen visible, and close the session together. The prompts below come from the source pack, grouped by file. Vocabulary: What other community helpers can you tell me about? What special equipment does a firefighter use? What does a nurse do all day? How do I add -ing to an action word? Grammar: Can you check my grammar answers? When do I use What and when do I use Who? Why do we say she is but they are? Reading and listening: Can you read the poem with me and explain it? Can you read the firefighter interview again? What is fire safety? Speaking: Can you help me practise describing jobs? Can you play Who Am I? with me? Story and writing: Can you read the story with me and explain any hard words? Can you check my sentences about the helpers on my street?

Begin the unit with the helpers the children actually see, so that every child can name somebody real. Keep the jobs open to all: show women driving buses and fighting fires, and men nursing and teaching. Some children have a parent in one of these jobs, and some do not, so invite stories rather than asking every child for one. When you mark, treat a missing helping word, such as the is in the nurse is helping, as the next step rather than as a mistake, and praise the child for choosing the right -ing spelling. The fire in the story is small and ends safely; if a child has lived through a fire, let them draw or talk instead of reading aloud. The unit value sits in Omar's last words: everyone has a job, and kindness is the job we all share.

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Firefighter — hose and ladder. Police officer — badge and whistle. Doctor — stethoscope and medicine. Teacher — books and board. Farmer — tractor and seeds. Accept the pairs written in either order.
1. fires, people. 2. safe, traffic. 3. ill, medicine. 4. teaches, learn. 5. crops, animals. The whole sentence must be copied out, not just the missing words.

1. True. 2. False; doctors help people who are ill, and farmers grow crops and vegetables. 3. True. 4. False; teachers teach students, and firefighters put out fires. Each false answer needs a corrected sentence.
1. helmets. 2. boots. 3. gloves. 4. mask. Note that the first three answers are plural because firefighters wear a pair or a set.
1. Who. 2. What. 3. Who. 4. What. 5. Who. Items 2 and 4 ask about an action and a thing, so they take What.
1. is helping. 2. are climbing. 3. is cleaning. 4. are learning. 5. is growing. Give one mark for the correct helping word and one for the correct -ing form.
Two of: putting out fires; rescuing people who are trapped inside burning buildings. Award the mark for any two correct jobs from the text.
Heavy boots, helmets, a mask and thick gloves. Accept three of the four for full marks, since the question in the source asks for at least three.
The mask is for breathing fresh air in smoke; the gloves are for holding hot things safely. Both halves of the question must be answered.
Look for four labels joined to the right body part: helmet to head, mask to face, gloves to hands, boots to feet. Spelling should be checked against the word list.
Grandmas, grandpas, mums, dads and children. All five are needed for the mark.
They are helping one another. Accept neighbours helping neighbours, taken straight from line two.
The repeated words are in my neighbourhood. The new line must keep the pattern and end with that phrase, for example: There are helpers waving in my neighbourhood.
firefighter. Accept a firefighter.
fire station. Do not accept school, since Leila only visits schools.
rides. Accept travels, but point out that the recording says rides.
water. One word is enough for the mark.
schools; fire safety. Both gaps must be filled for the mark.
Warta Street, with the market close by. Both details are in the opening sentence.
Karim is a window cleaner, and he uses a long pole. Both parts are needed for the mark.
Ten. Accept ten passengers or ten people on the bus.
Helmets, heavy boots, thick gloves and a mask. Three items earn the mark, four is a full answer.
Karim brought water; Nadia swept the floor; Rami carried the heavy boxes; the two firefighters helped clean the shop. Two named helpers with the right action earn the mark.
Because they helped him for nothing, after their own paid jobs were finished. Look for the idea of choosing to be kind, not simply doing a job.
The helpers are the window cleaner, the bus driver, the two firefighters, the police officer and the shopkeeper. The chosen job must be followed by a reason using because.
Anyone can help, and helping is not only for people who are paid to do it. Accept any answer that connects the saying to the street cleaning Omar's shop.
One example of the child helping and one of a neighbour helping the family. Both directions are needed for full marks.
A person who lives near you. The other three options define a firefighter, a shopkeeper and a helmet.
window cleaner. Only a person can hold a pole; helmet and uniform are clothing, and a market square is a place.
A mask. The badge and whistle belong to a police officer, and the tractor belongs to a farmer.
What is the police officer doing? An action takes What; the other three questions ask about a person.
are. Firefighters is more than one, so is and am are both wrong, and be has no -ing partner here.
Look for three separate sentences: one duty, one item of clothing, and one way the job helps the street. Check capital letters and full stops in all three.

Sample answers: What is the firefighter using? Who is holding the hose? What is the nurse doing? Who is the nurse helping? What is the farmer growing? Who is working in the field? Every question needs a question mark.
1. helping. 2. teaching. 3. rescuing (drop the e). 4. driving (drop the e). 5. cleaning. 6. running (double the n).
1. Karim is cleaning the window. 2. The firefighters are using water. 3. Amal is counting the passengers. Each sentence needs a capital letter and a full stop.
Six sentences, three with is and three with are, each naming a different helper and using a different -ing verb. Check that the helping word matches the number of people.
Six recorded questions: three beginning with Who and three with What, each answered in a full sentence. Listen for correct question order rather than perfect pronunciation.
Six spoken sentences, each with is or are and a verb ending in -ing. Mark the choice of is or are, and note any missing helping word as the next step.
A short role-play in which the child asks what happened, offers help, and answers a thank-you politely. Both roles should be tried before recording.
A heading with the job name, two duties, two named pieces of clothing or equipment, and a labelled drawing. The duties must be true of that job.
Five sentences covering where, who, what and the ending, in story order. Facts must match the story: the market, the fire engine, the two firefighters, the goats and the cleaning of the shop.
Six different promises, five opening with I can and the last with This week I will. Reject repeated promises, and check that the final one names a real action for this week.
Firefighter: hose, helmet. Doctor: stethoscope, medicine. Teacher: books, board. Police officer: badge, whistle. Farmer: seeds, tractor. Every helper must end up with exactly two items.
Six job-and-action pairs, each written as a full sentence with is or are and a verb ending in -ing, for example: The shopkeeper is stacking the shelves.
driving. The silent e in drive is dropped before -ing; drived and drivving are not words.
Ten. Amal counts aloud from one to ten and then says the total.
A long pole. The hose belongs to the firefighters and the tractor to a farmer, and no ladder is mentioned for Karim.
uniform. A helmet is firefighter equipment, and a tractor and a stethoscope are not clothes at all.
Saving someone from danger. The other options describe selling, growing and window cleaning.

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the people who live and work in my neighbourhood.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can describe a job and say what the person does.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can ask and answer questions with What and Who.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can count people and objects and say the number in a sentence.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can say what someone is doing now, using is or are with an -ing word.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself

selfAssessment.statement: I can name the special clothes and equipment for different jobs.
selfAssessment.scale: Not yet | With help | By myself
